extra.message: User-controlled username and password parameters are concatenated directly into a raw SQL query string passed to cursor.execute(). No parameterized query or input sanitization is used, allowing SQL injection to bypass login or exfiltrate data.

User-supplied 'server' parameter is directly interpolated into a shell command passed to os.popen(). An attacker can inject arbitrary shell commands (e.g., '127.0.0.1; cat /etc/passwd') achieving remote code execution.

The URL path segment is concatenated with 'static/' prefix and passed to os.path.join + open() without any sanitization. An attacker can traverse directories using '../' sequences (e.g., /static/../../etc/passwd) to read arbitrary files on the server.

The 'file' query parameter is concatenated with 'read/' prefix and passed to io.open() without any path sanitization. An attacker can supply '?file=../../etc/passwd' to read arbitrary files outside the intended 'read/' directory.

The 'query' parameter is rendered using {% raw query %} in HTML context, bypassing Tornado's auto-escaping. Combined with X-XSS-Protection disabled (server.py:88), user-supplied HTML/JavaScript is injected directly into the page enabling reflected XSS.

The 'link' parameter (set to user input at server.py:87) is rendered using {% raw link %} inside a <script> tag, bypassing all escaping. This allows JavaScript code injection without needing to break out of HTML, enabling reflected XSS in a script execution context.

Tornado application is running with 'debug': True enabled in production. Debug mode exposes detailed stack traces and application internals to end users, which aids attackers in reconnaissance and exploitation.

X-XSS-Protection header is explicitly set to '0', disabling the browser's built-in reflected XSS filter. This is a defense-in-depth measure being removed, making XSS attacks (present elsewhere in this app) easier to exploit.

Hardcoded database credentials (username/password pairs) are embedded in source code via INSERT statements. These include 'admin/admin', 'test-user/test@123', and 6 other accounts with plaintext passwords, allowing anyone with source access to compromise user accounts.
